title: The Boke of Curtesy
summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan CME00120, keyed from printed pp.58-63 of Karl Breul's article 'The Boke of Curtesy' in Englische Studien 9 (1886), pp.51-63. Breul's Middle English text is based on manuscript A, NLS Adv.MS.19.3.1, current ff.84v-86v, with five line positions supplied from E and variants from H, E, C, O, W, and P. His French parallel is transcribed from Pynson's P and collated with Wynkyn de Worde's W. The complete 526-page Internet Archive volume, complete OCR and metadata, the complete 482-canvas NLS digital object, and complete DIMEV v1.0 release are cached with fixity. DIMEV records nine witnesses, adding BL Add.8151 and a Wyer fragment to the seven sources known to Breul.
review_notes: The primary subject is the repository artifact CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00120.xml. The abstract work, witness objects, print article, facsimiles, U-M encoding, and later DIMEV catalog record are separate entities.

The U-M encoding starts at the edition heading partway down printed p.58 (PDF page 74) and continues through the French ending and article closer on p.63 (PDF page 79). It omits pp.51-57 and the source discussion at the top of p.58, as well as the rest of Englische Studien volume 9.

Breul prints 112 Middle English editorial positions. A lacks line 22 and lines 55-58, which he supplies from E, and has a later duplicate of line 37 that he declines to repeat; A therefore has 108 written lines, matching DIMEV's fifty-four-couplet description.

The English apparatus uses sigla A, H, E, C, O, W, and P. Breul inspected A, H, E, C, W, and P in stated ways; O evidence reached him through E. Hausknecht's O/W collation. Franck Arnold communicated the W variants used under the P-based French column.
The two XML DIV2 elements are parallel language versions, not numbered medieval work sections. The closing Cambridge date and Breul signature are modern article paratext.

Current NLS ff.84v-86v correspond to canvases and ZIP images 184-188. Canvas 183 closes Sir Amadas; 184 begins 'Lytyll chyldur'; 188 has the explicit followed by another hand's recipe on the same surface; 189 begins the following work.

The NLS Presentation Canvas identifiers for 184 and 188 returned HTTP 404. The recorded start and end IIIF Image API URLs returned unauthenticated HTTP 200 image/jpeg responses and are the actionable digital boundaries.
The NLS cache is complete relative to all 482 canvases in the provider manifest, not every physical leaf: the provider notes that numerous modern blank opening flyleaves were not all captured.

The repository root LICENSE is the software-worded Unlicense. It contains no XML- or data-specific scope statement, so applicability to CME00120 markup/data is recorded as unknown rather than broadened into a confirmed public-domain dedication.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00120
entities.description: The exact XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID all identify CME00120; the file is 38,912 bytes.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00120
entities.description: A public electronic text keyed from Breul's printed pp.58-63. It serializes the English and French parallel columns as consecutive language-scoped versions and retains apparatus, pagination, five-line milestones, italic typography, and the article closer.

entities.label: The Boke of Curtesy
entities.description: An anonymous Middle English didactic courtesy poem in rhyming couplets, with a late-medieval line-aligned Middle French translation in the O/W/P bilingual branch. It teaches children table manners, cleanliness, deference, speech, charity, and social conduct.
notes: Breul dates the work broadly to the mid-fifteenth century and probably the English Midlands; later work-specific confirmation was not established in this review.
The anonymous French translation is a work/version layer; Pynson's surviving copy is Breul's French textual base, not a demonstrated translator's exemplar.

entities.label: Karl Breul, 'The Boke of Curtesy' (1886)
entities.description: A thirteen-page article with witness descriptions, editorial rationale, and an A-based text. The edition supplies five positions from E, collates H/E/C/O/W/P, prints a P-based French parallel with W variants, and closes with Breul's Cambridge date of 12 June 1885.

bibliographic.citation: Karl Breul, 'The Boke of Curtesy,' Englische Studien 9 (1886): 51-63.
bibliographic.title: The Boke of Curtesy

notes: DIMEV's bibliography gives the whole article as pp.51-63; its witness-A edition entry gives pp.58-63, exactly the span represented by CME00120.
The completed volume is dated 1886; 12 June 1885 is Breul's article dateline, not its publication date.

entities.label: Internet Archive digital facsimile of Englische Studien 9 (1885-1886)
entities.description: The complete 526-page PDF scan of the bound ninth volume, accompanied by complete provider OCR and item metadata. Breul's article is printed pp.51-63, PDF pages 67-79; the encoded selection is printed pp.58-63, PDF pages 74-79.
entities.reproduction_format: PDF page images with text layer, plain-text OCR, and JSON metadata

entities.label: National Library of Scotland, Adv.MS.19.3.1 (Breul A)
entities.description: A paper miscellany copied about 1480 in a North-Midland context. Richard Heeg's Boke of Curtesy occupies current ff.84v-86v and ends with his signed explicit; a different hand's medical recipe follows on the same f.86v surface.

physical_description.extent: xii + 216 + xviii folios; Boke of Curtesy at current ff.84v-86v
physical_description.format: Bound quarto miscellany in nine fascicles
notes: Full red morocco binding dated 1964.
NLS attributes the principal hands to Richard Heeg and John Hawghton; this component is signed by Heeg.
The work begins on f.84v and ends on the shared f.86v surface before a later recipe in another hand.

entities.label: NLS digital facsimile of Adv.MS.19.3.1
entities.description: The complete 482-canvas object exposed by the NLS IIIF manifest. The provider notes that numerous modern blank opening flyleaves were not all captured. The target occupies canvases/images 184-188; canvases 183 and 189 were inspected as adjacent controls, and canvas 188 is a shared work boundary.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF page images and Presentation manifest

entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 541 (Breul H)
entities.description: A fifteenth-century paper manuscript whose Boke of Curtesy is discontiguous and fragmentary: Breul describes an opening on f.210r-v and continuation from verse 60 on f.207r-v, with physical loss from the torn f.210 leaf.

physical_description.extent: Breul: f.210r-v and, from verse 60, f.207r-v; DIMEV: f.210r-v, incomplete
notes: Exact current foliation and the discontiguous continuation require direct verification.

entities.label: British Library, Egerton MS 1995 (Breul E)
entities.description: A late-fifteenth-century paper manuscript with the poem at ff.58v-60r. Breul uses E to supply editorial positions 22 and 55-58 where A lacks text and collates it throughout.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library, MS Ee.4.35, Part I (Breul C)
entities.description: An early-sixteenth-century paper folio manuscript with 113 rows of the poem at ff.22v-23v. Henry Bradshaw's collation was printed by Furnivall, and Breul states that he checked the manuscript again.

physical_description.extent: Boke of Curtesy at ff.22v-23v; 113 rows reported by Breul

entities.label: Balliol College, MS 354 (Breul O)
entities.description: A sixteenth-century bilingual manuscript with an English-French Boke of Curtesy on pp.289-291. Breul's O readings came through E. Hausknecht's O/W collation rather than a claimed direct inspection by Breul.

notes: The manuscript gives interlinear English and French versions.

entities.label: Wynkyn de Worde, Here begynneth a lytell treatyse for to lerne Englysshe and Frensshe (Breul W)
entities.description: An early bilingual print, conventionally dated about 1497, with the Boke of Curtesy at ff.7r-10v. Breul directly transcribed W and used its English and French readings in his apparatus.

bibliographic.citation: Here begynneth a lytell treatyse for to lerne Englysshe and Frensshe ([Westminster: Wynkyn de Worde, about 1497]).
bibliographic.title: Here begynneth a lytell treatyse for to lerne Englysshe and Frensshe
bibliographic.pages: 12 leaves; Boke of Curtesy at ff.7r-10v

entities.label: Richard Pynson, A Lytell Treatyse for to Lerne Englysshe and Frensshe (Breul P)
entities.description: An early bilingual print dated about 1500, surviving without its title leaf. The Boke of Curtesy occupies ff.6v-10r. Breul collated it against W and uses P as the base of his printed French parallel.

bibliographic.citation: A Lytell Treatyse for to Lerne Englysshe and Frensshe ([London: Richard Pynson, about 1500]).
bibliographic.title: A Lytell Treatyse for to Lerne Englysshe and Frensshe
bibliographic.pages: 11 leaves; Boke of Curtesy at ff.6v-10r

entities.label: British Library, Additional MS 8151
entities.description: A manuscript witness at ff.201v-203v cataloged by DIMEV v1.0 but absent from Breul's seven-source account.

entities.label: A good boke to lerne to speke French (STC 24868)
entities.description: A fragmentary bilingual print conventionally assigned to Robert Wyer about 1533 and cataloged by DIMEV as a ninth witness; it was not among Breul's seven sources.

bibliographic.citation: A good boke to lerne to speke French ([London: Robert Wyer?, about 1533]).
bibliographic.title: A good boke to lerne to speke French

entities.label: E. Hausknecht's O/W collation for Breul
entities.description: The working collation of Balliol O against Wynkyn de Worde W communicated to Breul. It is modeled as an intermediary scholarly record so O readings are not falsely described as Breul's direct manuscript transcription.
notes: No surviving public copy or present repository for the working collation was established from Breul's article or DIMEV.

entities.label: DIMEV v1.0 record 3134 / IMEV 1920
entities.description: A versioned scholarly catalog record describing the poem, subjects, form, nine manuscript and print witnesses, their loci or extents, and relevant editions, including Breul1886b.
notes: DIMEV's witness-A entry describes fifty-four couplets and cites Breul pp.58-63; its bibliography describes the whole article as pp.51-63.
DIMEV lists H only at f.210r-v, while Breul also describes the continuation from verse 60 at f.207r-v.

agents.name: Franck Arnold
notes: Breul credits Arnold with communicating the W variants printed under the French text.

agents.name: Digital Index of Middle English Verse

work_portion.label: The Boke of Curtesy in Adv.MS.19.3.1
notes: The exact Image API start and end URLs returned unauthenticated HTTP 200 image/jpeg responses; corresponding Presentation Canvas IDs returned HTTP 404.
Canvas 183 closes Sir Amadas; 184 starts 'Lytyll chyldur'; 188 ends with 'Explicit quod Hyheg' and then a later recipe on the shared surface; 189 begins the next work.

Start member images/000184.jpg is 505,691 bytes, SHA-256 48cede7548a4015614f6efd76f14de4287347d9a91d0697b5847a0f685cc0040; end member images/000188.jpg is 548,288 bytes, SHA-256 0a773f6ff78daac82001519c176cc1e64b3bb5305077506af6570ced60be9914.
Coverage is complete relative to all 482 provider-published canvases; NLS states that numerous modern blank opening flyleaves were not all captured.

The mutable discovery HTML was not treated as a complete archival download; all directly used manuscript images and their canonical IIIF manifest are preserved in the complete cached bundle, while this current catalog URL preserves authoritative descriptive availability.

rights.component: Modern repository XML structure and markup; underlying medieval and editorial text excluded
rights.jurisdiction: Repository license assertion of unclear data scope
rights.basis: The pinned CME root LICENSE is the Unlicense, but it expressly speaks of software, source, and binary forms and supplies no path-specific XML or data scope statement. This review therefore records the assertion without treating CME00120 markup/data as conclusively dedicated to the public domain.
notes: Clarification from the repository maintainer would be needed before broad reuse claims for the markup/data layer.

rights.component: U-M electronic text and represented public-domain source material
rights.basis: The U-M item and collection apply No Copyright - United States to public content. This is a rights statement, not a license; provider service terms remain separate.

rights.component: Anonymous medieval Middle English poem and Middle French translation as historical textual works; physical objects and modern representations excluded
rights.basis: The work and translation are attested in fifteenth- and early-sixteenth-century manuscripts and prints and predate modern copyright by centuries. Physical ownership, modern markup, catalogs, and digital-image terms are separate.

rights.component: Breul's 1886 article, edition, apparatus, and typography; later scan excluded
rights.basis: The article was published in 1886 and is in the U.S. public domain because works published before 1931 are outside the current U.S. copyright term in 2026. Internet Archive metadata independently labels the carrier public domain.

rights.component: Internet Archive PDF scan, OCR, and metadata representation
rights.basis: The exact item metadata states 'In public domain. Work published prior to 1923.' That statement supports unrestricted underlying-work status but is not modeled as an affirmative open license for every provider-created file or metadata field.

rights.component: Underlying medieval Boke of Curtesy text embodied in Adv.MS.19.3.1; physical ownership and digital images excluded
rights.basis: The witness was copied about 1480 and the underlying anonymous text predates modern copyright by centuries. Physical-access and image-reuse terms are separate.

rights.component: NLS digital page images and item-level facsimile
rights.basis: The exact IIIF item metadata links Public Domain Mark 1.0 and states that the material is free of known copyright restrictions. NLS guidance says faithful copies retain the original status and waives related digitization rights with CC0 where they subsist; the exact item remains PDM-marked rather than licensed.

rights.component: DIMEV v1.0 data and project files
rights.basis: The complete versioned release README and LICENSE identify Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Breul's article pp.51-63 in a complete journal volume
editorial_practices.representation: Edition heading partway down p.58 through the closer on p.63
editorial_practices.description: The electronic text omits pp.51-57 and the source discussion at the top of p.58, then retains the Middle English text, French parallel, apparatus, and closer through p.63.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Breul's pagewise English text, French parallel, and footnote apparatus
editorial_practices.representation: Consecutive enm and fre DIV2 versions with duplicated PB 58-63 sequences and notes attached in their language stream
editorial_practices.description: The encoding preserves both versions and page references but serializes a parallel print layout sequentially. The French line alignment is represented by vertical bars inside six P elements, so element names alone do not define the intellectual form or line count.
editorial_practices.scope: Both version DIV2 elements on encoded pp.58-63

editorial_practices.source_feature: Seven English manuscript/print sources A, H, E, C, O, W, and P
editorial_practices.representation: A-based text with five E-supplied positions and footnote variants
editorial_practices.description: Breul abandons a fully reconstructed critical text in favor of A, correcting disruptive readings, supplying line 22 and lines 55-58 from E, bracketing A-only lines 95-98, omitting a later A duplicate of line 37, and recording the other versions in the apparatus. O reaches him through Hausknecht's collation.
editorial_practices.scope: Middle English text and apparatus, printed pp.58-63

editorial_practices.source_feature: Pynson P French text and Wynkyn de Worde W French text
editorial_practices.representation: P-based French column with nonorthographic W variants beneath
editorial_practices.description: Breul prints the French translation from P and records W variants communicated by Franck Arnold; this editorial source choice does not establish P as the translation's original exemplar.
editorial_practices.scope: French parallel and notes, printed pp.58-63

editorial_practices.source_feature: Abbreviations in witness A and other source readings
editorial_practices.representation: Expanded material in italics, retained as HI/@REND='italic' in XML
editorial_practices.description: Breul expands abbreviations and uses italic typography for supplied or editorially changed letters and passages. The XML preserves 643 italic HI elements, 435 inside notes and 208 outside notes; italics are editorial, not manuscript emphasis.
editorial_practices.scope: Text, French parallel, and apparatus on pp.58-63

editorial_practices.source_feature: A's spelling, thorn forms, u/v distinction, word division, and capitalization
editorial_practices.representation: A spelling retained in principle with stated normalization and italic marking of intervention
editorial_practices.description: Breul unfolds abbreviations, changes selected forms such as manuscript thorn-based pronouns to printed thou forms, separates u and v, and supplies capitals. The result is an edited witness text rather than a diplomatic transcription.

editorial_practices.representation: Modern editorial punctuation and numbered line positions
editorial_practices.description: Breul's punctuation and line arrangement are editorial. The 112 printed positions must not be mistaken for the 108 written lines physically present in A.

evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD, IDG, poem DIV1, English and French DIV2 versions, and closer
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for the repository artifact, identifiers, encoded source selection, 112 English positions, six French paragraphs, apparatus, page and line milestones, italic representation, and Breul closer.

evidence.citation: Kyvero-Press/CME, root LICENSE at pinned commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: The root Unlicense repeatedly describes software and source/binary forms but contains no path-specific XML or data scope statement; it supports recording the repository assertion and its ambiguity, not a broadened markup/data conclusion.
notes: No source-cache duplicate was made because this is an immutable file in the same pinned git repository as the tracked XML; the exact commit URL preserves availability and git show at that commit was inspected.

evidence.citation: Parent-aware structural and typographic audit of the exact CME00120 XML.
evidence.summary: Confirmed 38,912 bytes and git blob ebe01bb324ec05ab82f35f5743f2aaa9efea242d; 1 DIV1, 2 DIV2, 1 LG, 112 L, 6 P, 128 NOTE, 12 PB, 41 MILESTONE, 643 italic HI, 1 HEAD, 1 TRAILER, and one CLOSER/DATELINE/SIGNED. English/French notes are 105/23; 101 French bars delimit 107 nonempty display segments including omission notices.

notes: The dynamic item page remains available through an ordinary browser but returned an automated HTTP 403 during the final command-line audit. It was not cached separately because the exact derivative XML is the tracked repository artifact; both canonical item URLs preserve current metadata and rights availability.

notes: This mutable collection policy page was retained by canonical URL and access date rather than cached as if it were a fixed historical release; the exact work XML and complete fixed source editions are cached or tracked separately.

evidence.citation: Karl Breul, 'The Boke of Curtesy,' Englische Studien 9 (1886): 51-63, complete article in the Internet Archive volume PDF.

evidence.citation: Karl Breul, 'The Boke of Curtesy' (1886), witness and editorial discussion, pp.51-58.
evidence.summary: Breul's explicit account of A/H/E/C/O/W/P, direct versus communicated collations, A-based policy, five E-supplied positions, bracketed A-only lines, unprinted duplicate, abbreviation and spelling practices, and P/W treatment of the French text.

open_questions.question: What later linguistic and work-specific scholarship can confirm or revise Breul's mid-fifteenth-century, Midlands composition estimate?
next_steps: Consult recent editions and dialect studies.
Do not substitute witness A's c.1480 North-Midland copying context for the work's composition event.

open_questions.question: Who translated the poem into Middle French, when and where, and from which English version or exemplar?
next_steps: Collate complete O, W, and P bilingual versions and consult later phrasebook scholarship.
Keep Breul's P-based editorial French text distinct from the historical translation event.

open_questions.question: What stemma or redaction history connects all nine DIMEV witnesses?
next_steps: Consult post-Breul collations including Add.8151 and STC 24868.
Do not infer direct witness-to-witness derivation from Breul's edition-source relations.

open_questions.question: Does current BL foliation confirm Breul's discontiguous H continuation at f.207r-v in addition to the DIMEV-indexed f.210r-v fragment?
next_steps: Inspect Harley MS 541 directly or obtain current folio images.
Reconcile historical and current foliation before normalizing the complete H locus.

open_questions.question: Do Hausknecht's O/W collation or Arnold's W French-variant notes survive, and if so where?
next_steps: Search Breul, Hausknecht, and Arnold archival papers and correspondence.
Retain the intermediaries explicitly even while their present survival is unknown.

open_questions.question: Does the CME repository's software-worded root Unlicense intentionally apply to XML markup and data?
next_steps: Request a path-specific clarification from the repository maintainer.
Until clarified, preserve copyright status as unknown for the modern XML layer and do not broaden the software wording.
